findings.rationale: Both properties represent a quantitative size with square‑meter units, but our property refers to field area while the schema.org property refers to accommodation floor size.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/floorSize is specifically the floor area of an accommodation/building, whereas our term is the area of an agricultural field/plot; sharing square-meter units does not make them the same concept.

findings.rationale: eudr:areaSize specifies field area size, whereas gs1:grossArea refers to the overall item area including packaging.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:grossArea is the physical surface area of a product item including its packaging, whereas eudr:areaSize is the size of a geographic land plot/field of production, which are unrelated concepts despite both being areas.

findings.rationale: eudr:areaSize refers specifically to field area size, whereas schema.org/size is a general product dimension that can include many other measurements.
findings.qaRationale: eudr:areaSize is a QuantitativeValue for a geographic field's area (hectares/ares/m²), a specific instance of schema.org/size's general product-dimension concept, so ours is the narrower property.

findings.rationale: eudr:dueDiligenceStatement is a specific type of certification statement, whereas schema.org/hasCertification covers all kinds of certifications.
findings.qaRationale: An EUDR due diligence statement is an operator's self-declaration filed to the EU Information System, not a third-party certification of conformity (schema:Certification), so it is a distinct concept rather than a narrower kind of certification.

findings.rationale: Both properties link a subject to an authority, but the candidate is a general competent‑authority relation for public services, whereas our property specifically identifies the authority granting an exemption.
findings.qaRationale: The upstream property links a PublicService to its responsible PublicOrganization, whereas ours links a regulatory ExemptionDeclaration to the operator-or-authority asserting it (which need not be a competent authority at all), so the domains and the concept differ.

findings.rationale: Both properties describe geographic origin information, but our property is a list of detailed origins whereas the GS1 property represents a single country‑of‑origin statement.
findings.qaRationale: Our property is a list of structured origin-detail records (one per geolocation/polygon) whereas the GS1 property is a single textual country-of-origin statement, so ours is broader in structure though both concern geographic origin; the cardinality and range mismatch rules out EXACT/CLOSE.

findings.rationale: originList provides detailed origin information per location, whereas Has Geocoordinates only links to coordinate or shape data.
findings.qaRationale: originList is a list-valued container of structured EUDR origin records (country, dates, geolocation, etc.) on a Product, whereas gs1:geo merely links a Place to coordinate/shape data, so they are different concepts at different granularities rather than a narrower/broader pair.

findings.rationale: Our property is a list of detailed origin entries per geolocation or polygon, whereas the GS1 provenanceStatement is a single free‑text description of an origin area.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a structured list of geolocation/polygon-based origin-detail objects (EUDR due-diligence data), whereas gs1:provenanceStatement is a single free-text descriptive phrase about a general origin area, so they are not the same concept rather than a narrower form of it.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a date of a processing event, but transformationDate refers to commodity processing while productionDate refers to manufacturing or assembly.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties recording the date of a processing/production event on a product, but EUDR's transformation date is a regulation-specific commodity-processing concept that overlaps with but is not interchangeable with GS1's manufacturer production/assembly date.

findings.rationale: Both capture a date of a processing event, but transformationDate refers to commodity processing while productionDate denotes manufacturing or assembly.
findings.qaRationale: EUDR transformationDate marks when a commodity was processed/transformed (a downstream supply-chain event), whereas schema.org productionDate denotes original manufacture/production of an item, so they capture distinct points in the lifecycle rather than the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a date related to the creation or processing of a product, but transformation refers specifically to processing/transformation while production denotes manufacturing.
findings.qaRationale: Both are date-valued properties marking a creation/processing point, and overlap substantially, but transformation/processing of a commodity is not interchangeable with a manufacturer-determined production/assembly date-time, so they are close rather than exact.

findings.rationale: hasBatchLotNumber covers any batch or lot number for traceability, while exemptionScopeReference applies only to identifiers that are within a temporary exemption.
findings.qaRationale: exemptionScopeReference can hold a batch/lot identifier scoped to an exemption, but it also covers serial identifiers and is semantically tied to exemption scope rather than general traceability, making it a narrower, specialized subset of hasBatchLotNumber only when batch-scoped.

findings.rationale: exemptionType classifies exemptions as permanent or temporary, whereas employmentType covers a broader set of employment categories.
findings.qaRationale: An EUDR exemption classification (permanent/temporary) and schema.org's job employment type share only the 'temporary' surface token and are entirely unrelated concepts.

findings.rationale: Both properties record a date of an event related to compliance, but the specific events (statement submission vs audit completion) differ in scope.
findings.qaRationale: A regulatory due-diligence statement submission date and a certification audit completion date are distinct events on different domains, sharing only that both are dates.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent the same EUDR risk level for a product.
findings.qaRationale: The candidate IRI is identical to our own term's IRI in our namespace, so this is a self-reference rather than a genuine cross-vocabulary alignment and must be rejected.

findings.rationale: Derived From refers to any source product(s) used in creating the current product, while predecessorOf specifically links a discontinued variant to its newer version; thus our property is broader.
findings.qaRationale: derivedFrom expresses material/source provenance (this product was made from those source products), whereas schema:predecessorOf is a model-versioning pointer from an older product variant to its newer replacement; these are different concepts, not a broader/narrower pair.

findings.rationale: Derived From can refer to any source product(s) used in creation, whereas successorOf specifically links a newer variant to its earlier predecessor; thus our property is broader.
findings.qaRationale: successorOf expresses product-line/version predecessor lineage between model variants, whereas eudr:derivedFrom expresses material/source derivation of a product from input products; these are distinct relations and a successor is not a subtype of being materially derived-from, so there is no genuine hierarchical match.

findings.rationale: The schema.org code property is a generic medical coding attribute, whereas eudr:exemptionReasonCode refers specifically to exemption reason codes from the GS1 controlled vocabulary.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/code is a medical-domain coding property (ICD-9, SNOMED-CT, etc.) for describing medical entities, which is a different concept from a regulatory exemption reason code and not a generalization of it.

findings.rationale: The FSC Certification property refers specifically to Forest Stewardship Council certificates, whereas the upstream certificationIdentification is a generic reference to any product or party certificate.
findings.qaRationale: Both are certification-reference properties about FSC/generic certification, but ours ranges over gs1:CertificationDetails (the structured certification record) whereas upstream certificationIdentification is the certificate's identifier value, so they overlap in domain without ours being a clean subtype of the other.

findings.rationale: eudr:fscCertification refers to a specific FSC certification, whereas schema.org/certificationIdentification is a generic identifier for any certification.
findings.qaRationale: Ours points to a CertificationDetails certification record/instance (a class-valued reference to an FSC certification), while schema.org/certificationIdentification is a literal identifier string of a certification, so they denote different things (a certification vs. its ID).

findings.rationale: The FSC Certification property refers to a specific type of certification, whereas hasCertification is a generic property for any certification information.
findings.qaRationale: Our FSC-specific certification property is a narrower specialization of schema.org's generic hasCertification, and both are properties relating a product to certification information.

findings.rationale: eudr:fscCertification refers specifically to FSC certification, whereas gs1:Has Certification is a generic property for any certification.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties linking a product to its certification; ours is restricted to FSC certifications specifically, making it a strict specialization of gs1's generic certification property.

findings.rationale: eudr:fscCertification refers to a specific FSC certification, whereas gs1:certificationType denotes any type of certification.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a reference linking to a whole FSC certification record (range gs1:CertificationDetails), whereas gs1:certificationType is a classifier whose value names the kind of certification, so they are different concepts rather than broader/narrower of the same one.

findings.rationale: The property refers to a specific place where transformation occurs, which is a narrower use of the generic Location property.
findings.qaRationale: Our term is a property linking a product to its transformation place, whereas locn:Location is a generic location class, so a property cannot match a class and the concepts differ.

findings.rationale: eudr:areaHectares specifies area measured in hectares, whereas gs1:netArea is a generic area value that can use any unit; thus our term is narrower.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:netArea is the physical net area of a product item, whereas eudr:areaHectares is the size of a land plot/production area for deforestation due diligence — different concepts, not a units-only narrowing.

findings.rationale: Geofence specifies a polygonal area defined by longitude‑latitude coordinates, whereas geo is a general property for any geographic coordinate.
findings.qaRationale: Both express the geographic location of an entity and overlap on polygon shapes, but schema:geo links a Place to a structured GeoCoordinates/GeoShape object while eudr:geofence is a coordinate-array string on OriginDetails, so the encodings and domains diverge enough that this is overlapping rather than a clean narrowing.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a list of geographic coordinates that define an area, though the data format differs.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing geographic coordinates that delimit an area, but ours is a CBV-formatted polygon string scoped to plots >4ha while the upstream is a generic structured coordinate list for any geometry extent, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent a polygonal area defined by a series of coordinates, matching the same concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both encode a polygonal area, but they are not interchangeable: ours is a domain-specific geofence for EUDR origin plots with a coordinate-array serialization, whereas GS1 polygon is a generic geometry primitive using space-delimited points.

findings.rationale: eudr:geofence specifies a polygon coordinate array for large plots, whereas locn#geometry is a general property linking any resource to its geometry.
findings.qaRationale: Both are properties expressing the geometry of a location, and eudr:geofence is a constrained specialization (a coordinate-array polygon for plots >4ha) of locn:geometry's general resource-to-geometry association, so ours is narrower.

findings.rationale: Both properties represent geographic coordinates, but schema.org’s geo is intended for places while our geolocation is specific to OriginDetails.
findings.qaRationale: Both express geographic coordinates as a property, but schema.org's geo ranges over a GeoCoordinates/GeoShape object on a Place whereas eudr:geolocation carries a Geo URI string on OriginDetails, so they overlap without being interchangeable.

findings.rationale: eudr:geolocation is a single Geo URI for a plot, whereas m8g:coordinates represents a list of coordinates defining an extent.
findings.qaRationale: eudr:geolocation is a single Geo URI point/coordinate for one small plot, a specific case of m8g:coordinates which is a list of coordinates defining a geometry's extent, but the structural mismatch (single anyURI vs coordinate list) keeps this short of CLOSE.

findings.rationale: eudr:geolocation provides a full Geo URI with both latitude and longitude, whereas schema.org/latitude supplies only the north/south coordinate.
findings.qaRationale: schema:latitude is a single scalar coordinate component (a part of a location), not a hierarchically narrower kind of full geolocation point, so the relationship is partitive rather than a SKOS broader/narrower match; the correct upstream target would be schema:GeoCoordinates/schema:geo.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the effective start date of a condition (exemption vs price), sharing similar scope but differing in specific domain.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a regulatory exemption validity start date on an ExemptionDeclaration, whereas gs1:validFrom is defined specifically as the effective start date of a price, so the concepts do not match.

findings.rationale: eudr:exemptionEffectiveFrom denotes a general start date for an exemption, whereas gs1:productionVariantEffectiveDateTime refers specifically to the effective date of a production variant.
findings.qaRationale: An exemption's validity start date and a production variant's effective date are unrelated concepts sharing only the word 'effective'; one governs regulatory exemption applicability, the other governs which product variant applies based on package date marks.

findings.rationale: eudr:exemptionEffectiveFrom specifies the start date of an exemption, whereas schema.org/validFrom is a general property for any item’s validity; the former is a narrower, more specific instance.
findings.qaRationale: schema:validFrom is a generic validity-start date for any item, while ours is the same date concept but constrained to an exemption declaration's domain; from our term's perspective the upstream property is broader, so ours is narrower—NARROW—wait.

findings.rationale: eudr:exemptionEffectiveFrom specifies the start date of an exemption’s validity, a more specific instance of the general regulatory reference applicability start date.
findings.qaRationale: Both are property-typed applicability start dates, but ours is the start of an exemption's validity (a narrower, exemption-scoped case) versus the upstream's general start date for any referenced regulatory information.

findings.rationale: eudr:exemptionEffectiveFrom specifies the start date of an exemption’s validity, a more specific instance of the general referenced file effective start date.
findings.qaRationale: The GS1 term denotes when an externally-linked referenced file becomes effective for use (link/file metadata), whereas ours denotes the legal validity start of a regulatory exemption, so they are not the same concept despite both being start-date properties.

findings.rationale: eudr:mitigationMeasures covers all risk mitigation measures, while gs1:preparationConsumptionPrecautions is a specific subset of precautions applied before preparation or consumption.
findings.qaRationale: EUDR deforestation-risk mitigation measures in a regulatory risk assessment share no conceptual overlap with consumer-facing precautions before preparing or consuming a product; mere keyword similarity on 'precaution/mitigation' does not make them the same concept.

findings.rationale: Both properties provide information on measures to prevent or mitigate a risk, though one is general risk mitigation and the other focuses specifically on disease prevention.
findings.qaRationale: Our term concerns deforestation supply-chain risk mitigation under EUDR Article 10.2, while schema.org/diseasePreventionInfo is a medical-domain property about preventing illness; they share no conceptual overlap beyond superficial 'prevent/mitigate' wording.

findings.rationale: Both properties are dates indicating when a status becomes applicable, but eudr:deforestationFreeDate is specifically for deforestation‑free certification, whereas schema.org/validFrom is a general validity date.
findings.qaRationale: schema:validFrom marks when an item/offer becomes valid, whereas deforestationFreeDate asserts the date a deforestation-free status was confirmed against a regulatory cutoff; sharing the xsd:date range is not conceptual subsumption, so this is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: eudr:originDetails includes both geolocation and producer identification, whereas gs1:countryOfOriginStatement refers only to geographic origin.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern product origin and overlap on geographic provenance, but ours is a structured container (precise plot geolocation plus producer identification) while gs1:countryOfOriginStatement is a free-text description that also covers processing location, so neither cleanly subsumes the other.

findings.rationale: eudr:originDetails is a container that includes both geolocation and producer identification, whereas gs1:provenanceStatement only provides a free‑text description of geographic origin.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:provenanceStatement is a free-text human-readable origin description (e.g. 'Made in Napa Valley'), whereas eudr:originDetails is a structured container linking precise geolocation coordinates and producer identification, so they represent fundamentally different kinds of data rather than a broader/narrower form of the same concept.

findings.rationale: eudr:originDetails is a container for geolocation and producer identification, while schema.org/countryOfOrigin specifies only the country of origin; thus our term is broader.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern product origin, but ours is a structural container bundling precise geolocation and producer identification while schema:countryOfOrigin is a single coarse country-level value, so they overlap without one cleanly subsuming the other.

findings.rationale: eudr:originDetails is a container that includes both geolocation and producer identification, whereas gs1:countryOfOrigin only specifies the country code.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a structured container for plot-level geolocation and producer identity, which is a different concept from a single country-of-origin code rather than a broader form of it.

findings.rationale: eudr:volumeCubicMeters specifies a precise cubic‑meter measurement, whereas gs1:grossVolume refers to the overall volume including packaging and allows multiple units.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:grossVolume is specifically the overall volume including packaging, a distinct concept from EUDR's generic commodity volume measure, so the unit precision the first pass cited does not establish a subsumption relation.

findings.rationale: eudr:volumeCubicMeters is a general volume property for any product, whereas schema.org/cargoVolume applies specifically to cargo or luggage space.
findings.qaRationale: schema:cargoVolume denotes the capacity of a cargo/luggage compartment of a vehicle or container, not the physical volume of a timber/commodity product as eudr:volumeCubicMeters measures, so they are different concepts.

findings.rationale: eudr:producerIdentification covers any producer identifier, while gs1:globalLocationNumber is a specific type of identifier used for parties.
findings.qaRationale: Our property links to a producer Organization entity (range gs1:Organization), whereas gs1:globalLocationNumber is an identifier-key literal property identifying any location or party, so they are different kinds of concept despite the GLN preference note.

findings.rationale: eudr:countryList refers to a list of origin countries, whereas schema.org/addressCountry denotes a single country value; the former is a broader concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern country-of-origin/location, but eudr:countryList is a list of countries on a Product while schema.org/addressCountry is a single country value within a PostalAddress/Place, so ours is the broader (multi-valued, product-scoped) concept.

findings.rationale: eudr:countryList denotes a collection of origin countries, whereas gs1:addressCountry specifies a single country code; the former is a broader concept.
findings.qaRationale: gs1:addressCountry is the country component of a postal address, whereas eudr:countryList enumerates countries of origin/provenance of production geolocations; these are distinct concepts, not a broad/narrow pair.

findings.rationale: eudr:countryList refers to a collection of origin countries, whereas gs1:countryCode denotes a single country code; the former is a broader concept.
findings.qaRationale: Ours is a list-valued property modeling a collection of origin countries tied to EUDR geolocations, while gs1:countryCode is a single ISO 3166-1 code for an address, so they are not the same concept and a list does not subsume a scalar code.

findings.rationale: eudr:countryList specifies a list of countries, whereas gs1:countryOfOriginStatement describes any geographic area from which an item may originate, making the former a narrower concept.
findings.qaRationale: Both concern country/geography of origin and overlap, but ours is a structured list of countries while the gs1 term is a free-text descriptive statement of a geographic area, so they overlap without a clean genus-species containment.

findings.rationale: eudr:ActorRole is a general role for any actor in the supply chain, while schema.org/OrganizationRole is a specific subclass describing roles within organizations.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/OrganizationRole is a reification device for a time-bounded role an agent holds within an organization, not a classification of supply-chain function (operator/trader); the genuine broader match would be schema.org/Role, so OrganizationRole is not the same concept.

findings.rationale: eudr:ActorRole is a specific type of role within the EUDR supply chain, whereas schema.org/Role is a general-purpose role concept.
findings.qaRationale: schema.org/Role is a reification utility for attaching qualifying data (e.g. time period, character name) to a relationship, not a taxonomy of actor roles, so eudr:ActorRole is a different concept rather than a narrower subtype.

findings.rationale: eudr:ActorRole covers all actors in the supply chain, while schema.org/EmployeeRole is a specific type of role for employees.
findings.qaRationale: EUDR actor roles denote supply-chain participant functions (operator, trader) per Article 2, whereas schema:EmployeeRole models an employment relationship within an organization, so EmployeeRole is not a narrower kind of EUDR actor role but a different concept entirely.

findings.rationale: eudr:ActorRole is a specific role within the EUDR supply chain, whereas semic:Participation is a generic class for defining roles in any context.
findings.qaRationale: Both classes concern roles within a context, but semic:Participation reifies an agent's role-bearing involvement (the agent–role link) rather than the role itself, so eudr:ActorRole overlaps it without being a clean subtype, making NARROW too strong.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote the date when an item (exemption or other) ceases to be valid.
findings.qaRationale: schema:validUntil is a general-purpose validity end-date applicable to any item, whereas our term is narrowly scoped to the end date of an EUDR exemption declaration, making ours the narrower concept and the upstream term the broader one.

findings.rationale: Both properties denote an end date for a temporary condition, but one refers to an exemption’s validity while the other refers to product availability.
findings.qaRationale: Both are end-date properties but denote unrelated concepts—a regulatory exemption's validity period versus a product's commercial availability—so they are not the same concept.
